elimination_hints.misconception: Inventing plausible-sounding but incorrect expansions of the PASS acronym

elimination_hints.hint: Processing, Attention, Storage, Skills does not match the PASS model — 'Storage' and 'Skills' are not components; Simultaneous and Successive processing are.
elimination_hints.misconception: Substituting general cognitive terms for the specific PASS model components

elimination_hints.hint: Performance, Aptitude, Spatial ability, Speed are not PASS components — the model specifically comes from Luria's neuropsychological theory of cognitive functional units.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing standard psychometric categories with Das's neuropsychologically grounded PASS model

question_text: J.P. Das's PASS model of intelligence is rooted in the neuropsychological theory of:
explanation: The PASS model is based on Alexander Luria's (1966, 1973) neuropsychological theory of three functional units of the brain: (1) The brain stem and limbic system — regulating arousal and attention; (2) The occipital, parietal, and temporal lobes — coding (simultaneous and successive) information; (3) The frontal lobe — planning and regulating behaviour. Das mapped his four PASS processes directly onto these Lurian units, making the PASS model one of the few intelligence frameworks explicitly grounded in brain structure and function. The Cognitive Assessment System (CAS) was developed to measure PASS processes.
subtopic: Indian perspective — PASS model and Luria
subject: psychology

options.text: Alexander Luria's neuropsychological theory of three functional brain units
Sigmund Freud's psychodynamic theory of the unconscious
Jean Piaget's theory of cognitive developmental stages
B.F. Skinner's behaviourist theory of operant conditioning

elimination_hints.hint: Freud's psychodynamic theory concerns unconscious drives and personality, not cognitive processing or brain architecture — it has no connection to the PASS model.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing neuropsychological theories with psychodynamic theories

elimination_hints.hint: Piaget's theory describes developmental stages of logical thinking in children — Das's PASS model is grounded in Luria's adult neuropsychology, not Piagetian stages.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing cognitive developmental theory (Piaget) with cognitive neuropsychological theory (Luria)

elimination_hints.hint: Skinner's behaviourism focuses on observable stimulus-response learning — it is unrelated to the brain-based cognitive architecture that Das used from Luria.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying behaviourist theory to a neuropsychologically grounded cognitive model

question_text: The Indian perspective on intelligence as reflected in Indian psychology texts emphasises 'integral intelligence,' which goes beyond cognitive abilities to include:
explanation: Indian philosophical and psychological traditions conceptualise intelligence (buddhi) more holistically than Western psychometric approaches. Indian perspectives on intelligence include cognitive dimensions but also emphasise: (1) Emotional intelligence — managing relationships and feelings; (2) Social intelligence — understanding and navigating social contexts, including community and family obligations; (3) Spiritual intelligence — the capacity for ethical reasoning, self-transcendence, and alignment with dharmic principles. This 'integral' view contrasts with Western traditions that focus primarily on cognitive-analytical ability. Indian scholars have argued that Western IQ tests underestimate the full range of human intelligence relevant to the Indian cultural context.
subject: psychology

options.text: Only verbal and mathematical reasoning, consistent with Thurstone's PMAs
Exclusively creative divergent thinking as measured by the TTCT
General intelligence (g) and specific factors (s) as in Spearman's model
Emotional, social, and spiritual dimensions in addition to cognitive abilities

elimination_hints.hint: Indian psychology broadens intelligence beyond verbal and mathematical abilities — restricting it to Thurstone's PMAs is a Western-centric view that the Indian perspective explicitly expands upon.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying Western psychometric categories to the Indian holistic intelligence concept

elimination_hints.hint: Exclusive focus on divergent creative thinking is too narrow — Indian integral intelligence includes emotional, social, spiritual, and cognitive dimensions together.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reducing the Indian intelligence concept to a single cognitive process

elimination_hints.hint: Spearman's g and s model is a Western psychometric framework — Indian integral intelligence specifically moves beyond purely cognitive factors to include emotional and spiritual dimensions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Equating the Indian intelligence perspective with Spearman's factor-analytic model

question_text: Goleman's model of Emotional Intelligence includes five components. The component that refers to recognising and understanding the emotions of others is called __________.
explanation: Empathy is the fourth component in Goleman's (1995) model of Emotional Intelligence. It refers to the ability to recognise, understand, and share the feelings of others — to sense what others are experiencing and to respond appropriately. Empathy is considered essential for effective interpersonal relationships, leadership, counselling, and teamwork. It differs from sympathy (feeling sorry for someone) in that empathy involves truly understanding the other person's emotional perspective. High empathy enables more sensitive, attuned social interactions.
subject: psychology

options.text: Self-regulation
Empathy
Motivation
Self-awareness

elimination_hints.hint: Self-regulation is Goleman's second component — managing one's own emotions, not recognising others' emotions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing self-regulation (managing own emotions) with empathy (understanding others' emotions)

elimination_hints.hint: Motivation is Goleman's third component — intrinsic drive to achieve goals, not recognition of others' emotional states.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing motivation (internal drive) with empathy (understanding others)

elimination_hints.hint: Self-awareness is Goleman's first component — knowing one's own emotions, not understanding others' emotions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing self-awareness (own emotions) with empathy (others' emotions)

question_text: Cultural influences on intelligence are demonstrated by the finding that:
explanation: Cross-cultural research on intelligence has demonstrated that different cultures define, value, and develop different aspects of what they consider 'intelligent behaviour.' For example, some cultures emphasise social wisdom, cooperation, and practical knowledge over abstract reasoning. The concept of 'ecocultural' intelligence captures how environmental demands shape what cognitive skills are most developed and valued in a community. Flynn Effect research has shown that IQ scores have risen in many countries over generations, demonstrating that intelligence test performance is influenced by environmental and cultural factors like education, nutrition, and exposure to abstract thinking tasks.
subject: psychology

options.text: Different cultures define and value different aspects of intelligence, and IQ scores have risen across generations (Flynn Effect)
Intelligence is entirely genetic and identical across all cultures
Western definitions of intelligence are universally applicable without modification
Cultural factors only affect emotional intelligence, not cognitive intelligence

elimination_hints.hint: The Flynn Effect (rising IQ scores over generations) demonstrates that intelligence test performance is shaped by environmental and cultural change — not purely genetic.
elimination_hints.misconception: Accepting genetic determinism while ignoring the Flynn Effect evidence

elimination_hints.hint: Cross-cultural psychology has documented systematic cultural bias in Western tests — they are not universally applicable without cultural adaptation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming Western intelligence definitions are culture-neutral and universal

elimination_hints.hint: Cultural factors affect both cognitive and emotional intelligence — research shows that even 'g' loaded tests show cross-cultural score differences attributable to environmental variables.
elimination_hints.misconception: Restricting cultural influences to emotional intelligence while treating cognitive intelligence as culture-free

question_text: Which of the following BEST distinguishes Salovey and Mayer's ability model of EI from Goleman's model?
explanation: Salovey and Mayer's ability model conceptualizes EI as a genuine cognitive ability — a mental skill that can be measured with maximum-performance tests (like IQ tests), where answers can be objectively right or wrong. Goleman's model, in contrast, is a 'mixed model' that combines cognitive emotional abilities with personality traits (such as motivation, optimism, and social skills) and self-report measures. Critics argue that Goleman's model is too broad and mixes together distinct psychological constructs, making it harder to define and measure EI precisely. Salovey and Mayer's model is considered more scientifically rigorous and theoretically clean.
subtopic: Emotional Intelligence — ability vs mixed models
subject: psychology

options.text: Salovey and Mayer include more components than Goleman; Goleman's model is simpler
Goleman's model is ability-based; Salovey and Mayer's model includes personality traits
Salovey and Mayer's model treats EI as a measurable cognitive ability; Goleman's model mixes EI with personality traits
Both models are identical — they differ only in the names given to each component

elimination_hints.hint: Goleman's model actually has five components while Salovey and Mayer's has four branches — component count is not the key distinction, and the framing here is also backwards.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using component count as the defining difference between the two models

elimination_hints.hint: This reverses the actual distinction — it is Goleman's model that includes personality traits, while Salovey and Mayer's is a more pure ability-based model.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing which model is ability-based and which includes personality traits

elimination_hints.hint: The two models are fundamentally different in their conception of EI — one is ability-based (Salovey & Mayer) and the other is a mixed model (Goleman) incorporating personality characteristics.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating the two models as equivalent when they differ fundamentally in their theoretical approach

question_text: True or False: In Renzulli's three-ring model, a child with very high IQ but no creativity and low task commitment would still be classified as gifted.
explanation: This statement is false. Renzulli's Three-Ring Conception of Giftedness explicitly requires the intersection of all three rings — above-average ability, creativity, AND task commitment. A child with very high IQ alone does not meet Renzulli's criteria for giftedness if creativity and task commitment are absent. Renzulli argued against IQ-only definitions of giftedness and emphasised that high task commitment (motivation, perseverance) and creativity are equally essential. This model expanded the identification of giftedness beyond pure cognitive test performance.
subject: psychology

options.text: True
False

elimination_hints.hint: Renzulli's model requires all three rings — above-average ability, creativity, AND task commitment must all be present; high IQ alone is explicitly insufficient.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming that very high IQ alone is sufficient for classification as gifted under Renzulli's model

question_text: Which of the following is an example of 'simultaneous processing' in Das's PASS model?
explanation: Simultaneous processing involves integrating separate pieces of information into a unified, coherent whole at one time — it is holistic, spatial, and integrative. Examples include: understanding the spatial relationship between objects in a picture, comprehending the overall meaning of a paragraph (not just word-by-word), and solving matrix reasoning problems. In contrast, successive processing involves arranging information in a serial sequence (e.g., repeating a series of digits, following step-by-step instructions). Simultaneous processing corresponds to parietal and occipital lobe functions in Luria's model.
subject: psychology

options.text: Memorising a phone number by repeating it in order
Understanding a map by grasping spatial relationships between landmarks simultaneously
Focusing attention on a teacher's voice while ignoring classroom noise
Planning the steps needed to solve a multi-stage maths problem

elimination_hints.hint: Memorising a phone number in sequence is a classic example of successive processing — information is processed in a serial, ordered chain.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing simultaneous processing (holistic/spatial) with successive processing (serial/sequential)

elimination_hints.hint: Selectively attending while ignoring distractors describes the Attention-Arousal process in the PASS model, not Simultaneous processing.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing simultaneous processing with the attention component of PASS

elimination_hints.hint: Planning the steps of a multi-stage problem describes the Planning process in the PASS model — the executive, goal-directed regulation component.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing simultaneous processing with the planning component of PASS

question_text: Vandana is highly skilled at recognising when her colleagues are stressed or uncomfortable, adjusts her communication style accordingly, and helps resolve conflicts in her team before they escalate. She scores only average on standard cognitive tests. Which type of intelligence or ability does Vandana most clearly demonstrate?
explanation: Vandana's ability to perceive others' emotional states accurately, adjust her behaviour accordingly, and manage interpersonal dynamics effectively demonstrates high Emotional Intelligence — specifically, the higher branches of Salovey and Mayer's model (understanding and managing emotions) and Goleman's empathy and social skills components. Gardner's interpersonal intelligence also captures this ability. Her average cognitive test score highlights the classic finding that EI is largely independent of traditional IQ — people can have high EI with average cognitive ability and vice versa, supporting the distinction between cognitive intelligence and emotional intelligence.
subject: psychology

options.text: High fluid intelligence — the capacity to reason in novel situations
High bodily-kinesthetic intelligence — skilled use of the body in social settings
High Emotional Intelligence — perceiving, understanding, and managing others' emotions effectively
High analytical (componential) intelligence — logical analysis of social problems

elimination_hints.hint: Fluid intelligence involves abstract reasoning in novel cognitive tasks — Vandana's skills are specifically emotional and interpersonal, not abstract logical reasoning.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying fluid intelligence to social-emotional competence

elimination_hints.hint: Bodily-kinesthetic intelligence refers to physical coordination and motor skill — Vandana's abilities are emotional perception and social management, not physical.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing interpersonal emotional skill with bodily-kinesthetic ability

elimination_hints.hint: Analytical intelligence involves logical problem-solving with defined problems — Vandana's strength is emotional perception and social navigation, which standard cognitive tests do not measure.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reducing interpersonal social skill to analytical logical reasoning

question_text: Which of the following is a characteristic typically associated with creative individuals?
explanation: Creative individuals are characterised by: (1) Openness to experience — willingness to explore new ideas and unconventional approaches; (2) Tolerance for ambiguity — comfort with uncertainty and incomplete information; (3) Intrinsic motivation — doing things for the love of the activity rather than for external rewards; (4) Independence of judgment — willingness to challenge conventional thinking; (5) Risk-taking — willingness to pursue novel or unpopular ideas; (6) Wide range of interests; (7) Playfulness combined with persistence. Low tolerance for ambiguity and preference for routine are associated with lower creativity.
subject: psychology

options.text: High conformity and preference for following established rules
Low tolerance for ambiguity and discomfort with uncertainty
Exclusive reliance on logical deductive reasoning without imagination
Openness to new experiences, tolerance for ambiguity, and willingness to take intellectual risks

elimination_hints.hint: High conformity is associated with lower creativity — creative individuals tend to challenge conventional thinking, not follow established rules.
elimination_hints.misconception: Equating rule-following with creative productivity

elimination_hints.hint: Low tolerance for ambiguity is associated with lower creativity — creative people are comfortable with uncertainty and open-ended problems.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the relationship between tolerance for ambiguity and creativity

elimination_hints.hint: Exclusive reliance on logical deductive reasoning is characteristic of convergent, analytical thinking — creativity requires openness and divergent thinking beyond pure logic.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing logical-analytical thinking with creative thinking

question_text: Which of the following correctly summarises the distinction between 'aptitude' and 'achievement' tests?
explanation: Aptitude tests measure an individual's potential or capacity to learn a skill or domain in the future — they are predictive in nature. Achievement tests measure what an individual has already learned or mastered — they assess past learning and current knowledge. For example, an aptitude test for music assesses whether a person has the potential to learn music effectively, while a music achievement test assesses how well they can currently play or understand music. Both are used in educational and vocational settings, but for different purposes: aptitude for selection and placement; achievement for evaluation and certification.
subject: psychology

options.text: Aptitude tests measure current knowledge; achievement tests predict future learning potential
Aptitude tests predict future learning potential; achievement tests measure current knowledge and past learning
Both aptitude and achievement tests measure the same thing — the difference is only in test format
Aptitude tests are used only in clinical settings; achievement tests are used only in schools

elimination_hints.hint: This reverses the definitions — aptitude is about future potential (predictive), while achievement is about current/past performance (evaluative).
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the temporal orientation of aptitude and achievement tests

elimination_hints.hint: Aptitude and achievement tests are fundamentally different in what they measure (potential vs. acquired knowledge) and are not interchangeable despite some overlap.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating aptitude and achievement as measuring the same construct

elimination_hints.hint: Both aptitude and achievement tests are used across educational, clinical, and vocational settings — their use is not restricted by setting.
elimination_hints.misconception: Restricting each test type to a single setting when both are used broadly

question_text: The 'Planning' component of Das's PASS model involves:
explanation: Planning in the PASS model refers to the highest-level executive cognitive function — the ability to form intentions, select strategies, organise actions, monitor progress, and modify behaviour based on feedback to achieve goals. It maps onto the frontal lobe in Luria's neuropsychological framework. Planning is crucial for complex problem-solving, self-regulation, and goal-directed behaviour. Deficits in planning are associated with frontal lobe damage, ADHD, and certain learning disabilities. Das's Cognitive Assessment System (CAS) includes tasks like matching numbers, planned connections, and planned codes to assess planning.
subject: psychology

options.text: Forming intentions, selecting strategies, and monitoring behaviour to achieve goals
Selectively attending to relevant stimuli while ignoring irrelevant information
Processing information elements in a serial, step-by-step sequence
Integrating multiple stimuli into a single coherent pattern simultaneously

elimination_hints.hint: Selectively attending while inhibiting distractors describes the Attention-Arousal process in PASS, not Planning.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Planning with Attention-Arousal in the PASS model

elimination_hints.hint: Processing in a serial, step-by-step sequence describes Successive processing in PASS, not Planning.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Planning with Successive processing in the PASS model

elimination_hints.hint: Integrating multiple stimuli into a coherent pattern describes Simultaneous processing in PASS, not Planning.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Planning with Simultaneous processing in the PASS model

question_text:
Match the psychologist (Column I) with their contribution to the study of individual differences (Column II):
Column I: (P) Salovey & Mayer, (Q) Torrance, (R) Renzulli, (S) J.P. Das
Column II: (1) Three-ring model of giftedness — ability, creativity, task commitment, (2) PASS model of cognitive processing grounded in Luria's neuropsychology, (3) Four-branch ability model of Emotional Intelligence, (4) Tests of Creative Thinking measuring fluency, flexibility, originality, elaboration
explanation: Salovey & Mayer (P) developed the four-branch ability model of EI — perceiving, using, understanding, managing emotions (P-3). Torrance (Q) developed the Torrance Tests of Creative Thinking (TTCT) — measuring divergent thinking through fluency, flexibility, originality, and elaboration (Q-4). Renzulli (R) proposed the Three-Ring Conception of Giftedness — above-average ability, creativity, and task commitment (R-1). J.P. Das (S) developed the PASS model of cognitive functioning grounded in Luria's neuropsychological theory (S-2).
subject: psychology

elimination_hints.hint: P-1 is wrong: the three-ring giftedness model belongs to Renzulli (R), not Salovey & Mayer (P).
elimination_hints.misconception: Attributing Renzulli's giftedness model to Salovey & Mayer

elimination_hints.hint: P-4 is wrong: the Torrance Tests belong to Torrance (Q), not Salovey & Mayer (P).
elimination_hints.misconception: Attributing the Torrance Tests to Salovey & Mayer

elimination_hints.hint: P-2 is wrong: the PASS model belongs to J.P. Das (S), not Salovey & Mayer (P).
elimination_hints.misconception: Attributing Das's PASS model to Salovey & Mayer

question_text: Which of the following statements BEST reflects the 'inclusive education' philosophy for children with intellectual disability?
explanation: Inclusive education is the practice of educating children with disabilities, including intellectual disability, in regular mainstream classroom settings alongside typically developing peers, with appropriate support and accommodations. This philosophy holds that: (1) All children have the right to quality education in their local community school; (2) Diverse learners benefit from learning together — both students with and without disabilities gain; (3) With appropriate support (special educators, individualized education plans, assistive technology), most children with intellectual disability can learn and develop meaningfully. This contrasts with earlier approaches of segregating children with intellectual disability in separate special schools.
subject: psychology

options.text: Children with intellectual disability should be permanently separated into special schools to avoid disrupting regular classrooms
Only children with mild intellectual disability can ever benefit from any form of education
Children with intellectual disability should not be expected to develop any skills beyond basic self-care
Children with intellectual disability should be included in regular classrooms with appropriate supports and accommodations wherever possible

elimination_hints.hint: Permanent segregation contradicts the inclusive education philosophy — research shows benefits of integration for both children with disabilities and their typically developing peers.
elimination_hints.misconception: Accepting a segregationist model that inclusive education explicitly rejects

elimination_hints.hint: All levels of intellectual disability can benefit from appropriate education — even children with severe and profound levels develop skills with targeted educational intervention.
elimination_hints.misconception: Restricting educational benefit to mild intellectual disability only

elimination_hints.hint: Limiting expectations to self-care underestimates the potential of individuals with intellectual disability — many develop academic, vocational, and social skills with proper support.
elimination_hints.misconception: Adopting a deficit-only view that fails to acknowledge the development potential of individuals with intellectual disability

question_text: The concept of 'emotional regulation' in Salovey and Mayer's EI model refers to the ability to:
explanation: Emotional regulation (Branch 4: Managing Emotions) in Salovey and Mayer's model refers to the ability to monitor, regulate, and manage one's own emotions and the emotions of others to achieve goals and promote well-being. This includes the ability to stay open to positive and negative emotions, to reflectively engage or disengage from an emotion, and to use emotional information to guide adaptive behaviour. It is the highest and most complex branch because it requires integrating all lower-level EI skills — perceiving, using, and understanding emotions — into effective emotional management.
subject: psychology

options.text: Monitor and manage one's own and others' emotions to achieve goals and maintain well-being
Suppress all negative emotions to maintain a positive mood at all times
Recognise basic emotions like happiness and sadness in facial expressions
Understand the causes and transitions of emotions through emotional vocabulary

elimination_hints.hint: Emotional regulation does NOT mean suppressing all negative emotions — effective regulation involves being open to both positive and negative emotions and using them adaptively.
elimination_hints.misconception: Equating emotional regulation with emotional suppression

elimination_hints.hint: Recognising basic emotions in facial expressions describes Branch 1 (Perceiving emotions) — the most basic branch, not emotional regulation (Branch 4).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing emotional perception (Branch 1) with emotional management (Branch 4)

elimination_hints.hint: Understanding emotional causes and transitions describes Branch 3 (Understanding emotions) — one level below the management branch.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing emotional understanding (Branch 3) with emotional management/regulation (Branch 4)

question_text: A student successfully learns a new programming language very quickly, applies it to develop a novel app that no one in his class had thought of, and spends hours working on it without being told to. According to Renzulli's Three-Ring Model, this student demonstrates:
explanation: This scenario illustrates all three rings of Renzulli's giftedness model: (1) Above-average ability — quickly learning a new programming language demonstrates strong intellectual ability; (2) Creativity — developing a novel app that no one else conceived demonstrates creative thinking and originality; (3) Task commitment — spending hours voluntarily without external direction demonstrates intrinsic motivation and perseverance. The intersection of all three rings in a valued domain (technology) is precisely what Renzulli defines as giftedness in action ('gifted behaviour').

question_text: True or False: The PASS model by J.P. Das stands for Planning, Attention-Arousal, Simultaneous, and Successive processing.
explanation: This statement is true. The PASS model, developed by J.P. Das, Jack Naglieri, and J.A. Kirby (1994), identifies four cognitive processes: Planning — executive regulation and strategy formation; Attention-Arousal — selective attention and vigilance; Simultaneous processing — holistic integration of information; and Successive processing — serial, sequential processing. All four are grounded in Luria's three-unit neuropsychological model of the brain. The acronym PASS is derived from the first letters of these four processes and is a key topic in the Indian psychology curriculum.
